currentUse: Church building (Sixth & Poplar Sts.) last documented in secular use as a warehouse, partly derelict with broken windows; the former Holy Cross school at 320 W. Sixth St. is a collapsed ruin slated for borough demolition; the parish community was absorbed into Divine Redeemer Parish (Diocese of Harrisburg), which remains active.
summary: Holy Cross (Šv. Kryžiaus) church still stands at Sixth and Poplar Streets in Mount Carmel, but the Global True Lithuania heritage survey documents it as closed in 1992 and since used as a warehouse, partly derelict with many broken windows, its 1892 Lithuanian cornerstone still in place. The parish itself was merged with four other ethnic parishes in 1995 to form Divine Redeemer Parish, which remains an active Diocese of Harrisburg parish in Mount Carmel under Rev. Bernard Wamayose, AJ. The former Holy Cross school — successor building to the first Lithuanian Catholic school in the U.S. (opened January 6, 1908), its facade still bearing the Vytis — is in far worse shape: per a September 18, 2025 News-Item report, its roof and several floors have collapsed, and it was sold at a Northumberland County Tax Claims Bureau judicial sale on June 24, 2022 for $2,474.74 to Tamika Pressley, with the owner of record now Chocolate Nonprofits LLC of Montgomery County. On September 18, 2025 Mount Carmel Borough council voted to seize the school by eminent domain and plans to raze it using Community Development Block Grant funds. The parish's Sisters of St. Casimir legacy is actively commemorated: the Mother Maria Kaupas Center, a Divine Redeemer Parish ministry housed in the former St. Peter's Convent on West Avenue, marked its 10th anniversary with a Mass celebrated by Harrisburg Bishop Timothy Senior on May 18, 2025, and foundress Mother Maria Kaupas (whose congregation began its teaching mission at Holy Cross School in 1908) was declared Venerable in 2010, with her canonization cause ongoing.

developments.headline: Holy Cross merged into Divine Redeemer Parish
developments.detail: Divine Redeemer Parish was formed in 1995 through the merger of five ethnic parishes in Mount Carmel, including Holy Cross; the Lithuanian church building itself had closed in 1992 and passed into secular use as a warehouse.

sources.title: Who We Are | Mother Maria Kaupas Center
Shenandoah and southern Coal Region: Lithuanian heritage

developments.headline: Former Holy Cross school sold at county tax judicial sale for $2,474.74
developments.detail: Per the Northumberland County Tax Claim Bureau (as reported by The News-Item), the school property at 320 W. Sixth St. was sold to Tamika Pressley for $2,474.74 at a judicial tax sale on June 24, 2022; the owner of record became Chocolate Nonprofits LLC of Montgomery County, which averted a September 2024 upset-sale relisting by paying back taxes at the last minute.

sources.title: Mount Carmel Borough to invoke eminent domain to acquire former school building

developments.headline: Mother Maria Kaupas Center marks 10th anniversary
developments.detail: The Kaupas Center — a Divine Redeemer Parish ministry honoring the foundress of the Sisters of St. Casimir, housed in the former St. Peter's Convent on West Avenue — celebrated ten years with a Mass on May 18, 2025, with Bishop Timothy Senior of Harrisburg as main celebrant. The Center runs college-student service stipends, food pantry, nursing home and library volunteering, keeping the Kaupas/Holy Cross legacy alive in Mount Carmel.

sources.title: 10th Anniversary of the Mother Maria Kaupas Center in Mount Carmel
Who We Are | Mother Maria Kaupas Center

developments.headline: Borough votes to seize collapsed Holy Cross school by eminent domain, plans demolition
developments.detail: Mount Carmel Borough council directed its solicitor to take the former Holy Cross Roman Catholic School (320 W. Sixth St.) by eminent domain: the roof and several floors have collapsed, an adjacent alley and sidewalk are closed, and the borough manager said 'there's no second or third floor.' The plan is to acquire the deed, then use two years of Community Development Block Grant funding plus Housing Authority of Northumberland County assistance to raze the structure. The vote was 5-1.

conflictsWithArchiveRecord: No contradictions; three refinements. (1) Archive note "School building tax-sold 2022" is confirmed and sharpened: judicial tax sale June 24, 2022, price $2,474.74, buyer Tamika Pressley, owner of record now Chocolate Nonprofits LLC — and it has been overtaken by events: the borough voted Sept 18, 2025 to seize the school by eminent domain for demolition. (2) Archive note "Home of the founding of the Sisters of St. Casimir (1907)" needs nuance: the Sisters of St. Casimir state the congregation was founded in 1907 in Scranton, PA; Mount Carmel's Holy Cross was where the first three sisters arrived in October 1907, the site of the congregation's first motherhouse (Holy Cross Convent) and first mission — Holy Cross School, the first Lithuanian Catholic school in the U.S., opened Jan 6, 1908. (3) Closure timeline: the church building closed in 1992 (Global True Lithuania) while the canonical parish merger into Divine Redeemer occurred in 1995 as a five-parish (not simple absorption) consolidation — consistent with the archive but worth stating precisely.
gaps: The church building's CURRENT (2026) condition and ownership could not be verified: the "warehouse, partly derelict, broken windows" description comes from Global True Lithuania's undated heritage survey (its US fieldwork is largely mid-2010s), and no county deed record, sale price, or buyer for the church building after the 1995 merger was found in accessible sources. No press coverage of the church building itself from 2020-2026 was found. The Divine Redeemer Parish history page (divineredeemerpa.weconnect.com/Holy-Cross-) was "temporarily unavailable" at fetch time — its founding/merger details here come from search-index excerpts of that page corroborated by the Kaupas Center site. Direct page fetches were blocked mid-research by a tool permission restriction for several sources (Wikipedia disambiguation confirmed the en.wikipedia "Holy Cross Lithuanian Roman Catholic Church" article is about Dayton, OH — not this parish). Whether the school demolition has actually proceeded since the Sept 2025 eminent-domain vote is unknown as of July 2026. Identity confirmation: Holy Cross was Mount Carmel's only parish of that dedication, located at Sixth and Poplar Sts. and consistently described as the Lithuanian parish, so the match is not ambiguous.
